rules.sourceTitle: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015
rules.notes: This standard specifies the varieties, scope of use, and maximum dosages of food additives. Specific limits must be verified by consulting the full document.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015

rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives in all foods. Provisions are available in a searchable online database.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods... This database provides, in a searchable format, all the provisions for food additives that have been adopted by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, representing the maximum concentration legally permitted to protect human health.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Refer to the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) for specific maximum levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXS 193-1995 General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed

rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Refer to the Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices (CXC 78-2017).
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Food additives must be approved for use in the EU and comply with their conditions of use as listed in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites must comply with Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: For Ready-To-Eat (RTE) foods, shelf-life studies (durability studies and challenge tests) for Listeria monocytogenes must be conducted according to technical guidance, and sampling procedures for L. monocytogenes in processing areas and equipment must follow guidelines to comply with food safety criteria in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Technical guidance document on shelf-life studies for Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods provides specialised laboratories with detailed and practical information on how to conduct shelf-life studies

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Process hygiene criteria must be met to indicate the correct functioning of the production process, as laid down in Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: this Regulation lays down certain process hygiene criteria to indicate the correct functioning of the production process.

rules.foodCategory: Certain live animals and products (e.g., poultry, meat, eggs)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: For trade of certain live animals and products into Sweden, Finland, and Norway, special guarantees regarding Salmonella prevalence and control programs must be met, as laid down in specific Council Decisions and Regulations. Other EU countries can obtain similar guarantees if their control program is recognized as equivalent.
rules.evidenceQuote: When Sweden and Finland joined the EU special guarantees on Salmonella were provided as regards trade from other countries of certain live animals and products.

rules.limit: Verification required using "Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives"
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Different limits apply depending on the additive and food product type.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.limit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: General requirement for all food products sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: General requirement for all food products sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.limit: Must have technological justification and not present health risks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Criteria for allowing food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.limit: Verification required using SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Applies to all food products before import or sale.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Applies to all food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: If a food product contains an additive that is not permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.limit: Food businesses must be aware of commonly found contaminants and conduct laboratory tests as needed
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Guidance for compliance.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.limit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: General requirement for all food sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: If laboratory test results indicate contaminants exceed limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.parameter: Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues
rules.limit: Must comply; verification required from SFA website

rules.notes: SFA has established MRLs for compliance by food businesses.

These MRLs can be downloaded from the SFA website at the following weblink.

rules.parameter: Microbiological standards, heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues, other incidental constituents

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food

rules.sourceTitle: Food Additive Listings; Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS)
rules.notes: Food additives and color additives must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74), be Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS) (21 CFR Parts 182, 184), or be prior-sanctioned (21 CFR Part 181). Prohibited substances are listed in 21 CFR Part 189. The 'Substances Added to Food inventory' is a partial list.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively), and flavoring substances evaluated by FEMA* and JECFA*. Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184).

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens recognized by U.S. law are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. Sesame was added as the 9th major allergen effective January 1, 2023, by the FASTER Act.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Guidance Level for 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Action Level for total aflatoxins in general foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Action Level for total aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Peanuts and peanut products Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.375

rules.parameter: Chemical contaminants (tolerances, action levels, guidance levels)
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Contaminant levels are published in various guidance documents and regulations for industry. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 CFR parts 109 and 509. The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Contaminant levels listed in the transparency tool include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels... Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Advisory statements (e.g., 'may contain [allergen]') are not required by law. They can be used to address unavoidable cross-contact only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing practices and taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact. Such statements must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Consumers may also see advisory statements such as “may contain [allergen] or “produced in a facility that also uses [allergen].” Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact

rules.parameter: Declaration of Cochineal extract and carmine
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, identified as allergenic substances, must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: The food source of all major food allergens must be declared. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') or immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.'). The type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must also be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The law requires that food labels identify the food source of all major food allergens used to make the food. This requirement is met if the common or usual name of an ingredient already identifies that allergen's food source name... The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is equal to or greater than 10 parts per million total sulfites.
rules.evidenceQuote: Various sulfiting agents, including sodium bisulfite, are allowed as food ingredients. But due in part to adverse reactions to them, such as asthma in sensitive individuals, they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling. On August 12, 2020, compliance requirements were established for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the 'gluten-free' claim.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.evidenceQuote: Hệ thống văn bản
